title: The Descent
html:
Soo-Yun's first rock leaves her hand at the same moment my boots hit the ground at a full run.

The Thwack! is audible from 10 meters and the near kappa's bowl empties sideways, arm dropping mid-raise. I close the last 3 meters and the machete comes down before the kappa fully folds.

The second kappa is already stepping to cut off Soo-Yun's angle on the third. It's deliberate: the kappa moves between her and the target before she can reload, using its body as cover. I don't have time to get to it before it's set.

Jae-won gets there first.

He steps out of his planted position and drives his shoulder into the kappa's ribs with a Thud! that sends them both 2 meters sideways into the tree line. The bowl tilts with the impact. Mi-rae's first creek stone finds the temple before the water can settle back, and the spill finishes it.

That leaves 2. The third kappa is coming for Jae-won while he recovers his footing, machete already up. I get there in 4 steps and put the flat of the blade against its forearm, redirecting the swing away from Jae-won's head and into the dirt, then follow through to the bowl in one motion. The water empties in a pour and the kappa buckles.

Soo-Yun's third rock carries the length of the column and connects with the fourth kappa's temple with a flat Thwack! from behind. The kappa is mid-swing at Se-ah when it hits. The bowl tilts. Se-ah steps into the stumble and drives the kappa's head down into the platform stones with both hands.

Her jacket is still over one arm. She never put it down.

I finish the last kappa and straighten up. My arms are burning from the elbow down. The creek water in my clothes has had enough time to go from cold to just a weight I'm carrying. The gate is 40 meters ahead.

---

Tutorial Complete!

Time Remaining: 41 minutes.

Participants Reaching the Exit: 61 of 102.

---

---

Top Participant — Kill Count: [Eric / Alias]

Kills: 7

Achievement Unlocked: First Blood

First participant to engage a hostile entity in Tutorial Cycle 7.

Reward: +1 to all base stats. 500 bonus EXP.

---

---

[Alias: Eric]

Level: 4

Class: Scavenger

HP: 135 / 135 (restored)

Agility: 18

Perception: 15

Strength: 12

Endurance: 13

Available Skill Points: 6

---

61 of 102. I stand over the dead kappa and do the subtraction without wanting to.

The gate is still active between the columns. Groups are already filtering toward it from across the exit zone, reading their notifications in silence, some still watching the tree line. The wider group that had attached to our column moves with us. Nobody needs to be told.

"The 41," Soo-Yun says quietly.

"Tutorial zone closes at zero," I tell her. "Anyone still inside—"

"I know what it means."

She does. I stop talking.

The gate crossing is a pressure that goes from the scalp to the soles of the feet in less than a second: cold without wet, weight without mass, and then it's done and the forest is gone.

What's on the other side is open sky, pale blue, with a sun sitting at roughly 2 o'clock that throws light a few degrees warmer in color than the tutorial zone ever managed. The ground is a wide stone platform, flat and clean, ringed by a low barrier at the edge.

Below the barrier, a long way below it, is a city.

A functioning city, dense and spread wide across a hillside, architecture stacked in tiers that climb on 3 sides. Stone with a material between the courses that I don't have a name for. Smoke from chimneys. Movement in the streets. People already living in it, which means people who've been here long enough to build chimneys and fill streets.

The 61 of us stand on the platform above it in the same clothes we were transported in, with whatever the tutorial gave us and nothing else.

Nobody says anything for a moment.

A point of light appears above the platform.

It drops fast, slowing as it nears the stone, and resolves into a shape that reads as a person except for the wingspan folded against its back and the fact that the light is coming from the figure itself, not from anything above it. She lands without sound and folds the wings flat in one motion, then faces the group of 61 with the expression of someone for whom the number on the platform is the only detail worth checking.

She's close to 2 meters tall in something between armor and cloth that doesn't sit right against the physics of the platform. Her face is built along human proportions but wrong in the specifics, the geometry of it slightly off in a way that takes a second to place. Her eyes don't reflect the sun.

"Welcome to Eoran," she says. Her voice carries across the platform without volume. "You have completed the tutorial. Congratulations."

Nobody moves.

"I see a lot of you still passed," she adds.

The way she says it carries no warmth and no criticism. She's noting an unexpected figure. A deviation from the average, the same way a technician notes a reading that's higher than the baseline.

"This cycle's tutorial zone was lenient," she continues. "The kappa population ran 40% below the standard seeding. You had favorable conditions."

A man near the back of the group speaks up. Mid-twenties, broad shoulders, the kind of accent that comes from somewhere in eagle country. He asks it like someone asking a question they already know the answer to.

"What happened to the other 41?"

"The tutorial zone closes at the expiry of the allocated time," the angel says. "Any participant inside the zone at closure does not persist. This is communicated in the intake briefing."

"There was no briefing," he says.

"There was," she says. "You were not conscious for it."

The quiet that comes after that is different from the quiet after the notification appeared. The notification was a number. This is a person telling them the number was people, and that the system planned for it, and that whoever designed the intake thought that was an acceptable way to communicate the rules.

A woman somewhere to my left makes a sound that isn't a word. Someone else, a younger voice, asks something in a language I don't catch. The heavyset man from the creek bank is standing 3 meters from me and his face has gone the color of the platform stones.

The angel waits. Not for the group to settle. She's tracking something only she can see, eyes moving through a fixed point in the air above the crowd.

"Eoran has been receiving participants for 6 cycles before yours," she says, when the group noise drops below a threshold she apparently has in mind. "Approximately 2,400 individuals are currently established in the world. They arrived in earlier cycles. They did not come as your allies and they are not waiting to become them. Resources in Eoran are finite and contested. You will compete for them."

She pauses, eyes still on the fixed point.

"I advise against clustering near this platform. Previous arrivals are aware of the descent point."

The man from eagle country asks what that means, specifically.

The angel looks at him directly for the first time.

"It means they have had time to prepare for your arrival," she says. "How they have chosen to use that time varies by faction."

She doesn't add anything to that. She spreads the wings once, a single motion that moves air across every person on the platform, and lifts. By the time anyone in the group thinks to call after her she's already 30 meters up and not slowing.

She doesn't look back.

The 61 of us stand above Eoran and the city below doesn't care that we're here. The smoke from the chimneys keeps rising. The movement in the streets continues. Somewhere down there, 2,400 people are already going about whatever their lives in this world look like, and the angel just told us they know we arrived.

Jae-won is at the platform barrier, looking down at the streets. Mi-rae is still watching the sky where the angel went, which is more information about her than anything she's said in the last hour. Se-ah has already moved to the platform's perimeter and is walking the edge, reading it, checking angles.

The man from eagle country is standing where he stopped, staring at the space where the gate was. A woman beside him, smaller, jacket with a panda country university logo across the chest, puts a hand on his arm. He doesn't look at her.

I look at the platform edge. A staircase is cut into the stone on the south side, the steps worn smooth from previous arrivals.

'First ones down pick the ground.'

"Move," I tell the group. "South stairs. Before anyone with a 6-cycle head start decides to come up and welcome us themselves."

Se-ah is already at the top of the staircase.
